bibliographic_data.title: Slowing-down reduction and Possible Reversal Trend of Tropospheric NO2 over China during 2016 to 2019
bibliographic_data.abstract: Atmospheric nitrogen dioxide (NO2) over China at national level has been kept reducing since 2011 as seen from both satellite observations, ground-based measurements and bottom-up emission inventory. After 2016, however, a significant slowing-down of the reduction trend and/or even a reversal trend were found in numerous provinces, particularly in those with heavy NO2 levels, based on satellite observations. Error analysis on satellite data excluded cloud contamination, instrument anomalies from the main reasons of this change. Ground-based measurements show strong positive correlations with satellite observations and similar patterns of year-to-year changes of NO2 in 2018 and 2019 winter time. The temporal variations of Satellite NO2 over China are believed largely determined by surface emission from power plant and transportation. The reason for the recent change from emission perspective cannot be traced down since the national emission inventory was not updated since 2015. We therefore call on immediate attentions from both scientist community and policy makers to this phenomenon. Further efforts should be made to understand the reasons causing this change and to make associated air pollution controlling actions.

core_content.novelty_claim: The study identifies a significant slowing-down of the reduction trend, and even a reversal in NO2 levels over China from 2016 onwards, based on satellite observations and ground-based measurements.
core_content.key_methodology: The research utilized both satellite-derived NO2 column density data (from 2005-2019) and ground-based NO2 concentration data (from 2015-2019) to analyze the trend of NO2 in recent years. The temporal variations were attributed to surface emissions from power plants and transportation.
outcomes: A significant slowing-down or reversal of NO2 reduction trends was observed after 2016, particularly in heavily polluted regions.
The ground-based measurements showed strong positive correlations with satellite observations and similar patterns of year-to-year changes.
core_content.full_text_markdown: This study investigates the trend of tropospheric nitrogen dioxide (NO2) over China from 2005 to 2019. The research combines satellite-derived NO2 column density data and ground-based NO2 concentration measurements to understand recent trends. Initially, NO2 levels decreased significantly from 2011 to 2015 due to strict emission control measures. However, after 2016, the reduction rate slowed down or even reversed in many provinces, especially those with high initial NO2 levels. The study attributes this change to surface emissions from power plants and transportation, but notes that the national emission inventory was not updated since 2015, making it difficult to trace the exact cause.
